paragraphs: Despite a tremendous amount of research on the topic, we still have little evidence regarding the extent to which transformational leader behaviors (TLBs) cause a number of outcomes. The primary inhibitors include a lack of theoretical precision, the conflation of leader (follower) behaviors with evaluations, as well as measurement and design issues which prevent causal inferences. To address such concerns, we reframe the transformational leadership literature from a signaling theory perspective. Study 1 reviewed existing definitions of transformational leadership. Building on this, we introduce a new definition of TLB: Leader signaling through developmental and prosocial behaviors tailored for each unique stakeholder (e.g., person, dyad, group, organization). Leveraging topic modeling, Study 2 involved the analysis of open-ended survey responses. Using a constant comparative approach, six TLBs were identified: 1. teaching life lessons, 2. introduction to developmental opportunities, 3. providing different perspectives, 4. seeking different perspectives, 5. questioning critical assumptions, and 6. speaking words of affirmation. Studies 3 and 4 were preregistered experiments that showed TLBs cause variation in follower evaluations of the leader as transformational (n = 416; Cohen's d = .50) and contributions to a public good (n = 320; Cohen's d = .36), respectively. We conclude with recommendations for theory and practice.

Transformational leadership has been the focus of over 15 metaanalytic reviews (e.g., Bono and Judge, 2004;Lowe et al., 1996) as well as the topic of courses and books offered by consultants around the world (e.g., Crowley, 2011;Medcalf and Gilbert, 2017). Transformational leadership is unquestionably one of the most dominant leadership paradigms in the organizational sciences (Banks, Gooty et al., 2018;Dinh et al., 2014). However, despite the tremendous amount of research, three concerns exist that provoke questions of whether transformational leadership simply captures whether one likes their boss (Yammarino et al., 2020) or is just another positive leadership theory (Alvesson, 2020;Alvesson & Einola, 2019).

Problematically, the lack of conceptual clarity, theoretical and empirical conflation between leader behaviors and follower evaluations, and overreliance of questionnaires prevent transformational leadership from having a legitimate impact on leadership in practice. First, definitions of transformational leadership often included tautologies (i.e., a leader is only transformational when transformation occurs) as well as significant, if not complete, overlap with charismatic leadership. Second, the conflation of transformational leader behaviors (TLBs; x 1 ) and followers' evaluations (y 1 ) muddles the individual impact of each, combining the followers' subjective evaluation of the leader with their retroactive assessment of the leader's behavior (Martinko et al., 2018). Consequently, the empirical findings related to TLBs are conflated with evaluations of the followers' reporting on such behaviors (x 1 ≠ y 1 ), and are likely spurious due to endogeneity bias (for reviews of endogeneity bias see Antonakis et al., 2010;Hill et al., 2021). Third, the study of transformational leadership has over-relied on questionnaires. Although the evaluations captured through these questionnaires are theoretically important (y 1 ), continuing to build leadership theory only on questionnaires that measure endogenous variables will provide modest advancements at best for theory and practice (Alvesson, 2020;Antonakis et al., 2016).

The current manuscript reviews these three critiques of transformational leadership in more detail. However, to offer a contribution beyond existing critiques (e.g., Alvesson & Einola, 2019;Siangchokyoo et al., 2020;van Knippenberg & Sitkin, 2013), we https://doi.org/10.1016/j.leaqua.2022.101632 Received 22 December 2020; Revised 24 June 2022; Accepted 28 June 2022 Available online 22 July 2022 1048-9843/© 2022 Elsevier Inc. conducted four studies that improve both the theoretical conceptualization of transformational leadership and the way in which it is empirically studied. The first study conducted a review of transformational leadership measures and associated definitions to investigate key components of the concept as well as to develop a definition of TLBs (Study 1). Then, applying topic modeling combined with a constant comparative approach, Study 2 identified six TLBs. Studies 3 and 4 then experimentally manipulated these behaviors to evaluate their observability and the extent to which they actually transform followers' behavior. The overall aim is to put leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership research, which improves the theoretical specificity and methodological rigor to create new avenues for future research (e.g., allowing for data science techniques).

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: A good definition of leader behaviors requires specific attributes in order to distinguish one leader concept from another (Podsakoff et al., 2016). Transformational leadership has been traditionally conceptualized as multi-dimensional, consisting of four attributes (Bass, 1985). The first attribute idealized influence, or charisma, captures the degree an individual engages in behaviors that encourage followers to identify with the leader. Second, inspirational motivation involves stimulating enthusiasm as well as building followers' confidence in their ability to successfully perform assignments and attain group objectives (Yukl & Van Fleet, 1982). Third, intellectual stimulation entails raising problem awareness and promoting problem solving behaviors, in part, by altering the way followers view their beliefs and values. Lastly, individualized consideration is the provision of socio-emotional support concerned with developing and empowering followers (Bass, 1985). Problematically, however, Banks and colleagues (2017) demonstrated that the two of the four attributes, idealized influence and inspirational motivation, overlap with Antonakis et al.'s (2016) definition of charismatic leadership (value-based, symbolic, and emotion-laden leader signaling). It is likely the incoherence of these attributes that has led to such overlap and confusion (e.g., Alvesson & Kärreman, 2016;van Knippenberg & Sitkin, 2013;Yukl, 1999). Consequently, greater clarity is needed regarding the attributes of TLBs to determine how they are distinguishable from other concepts of leadership.

A good definition also avoids making a tautological statement (MacKenzie, 2003;Suddaby, 2010). A tautological statement restates in slightly different words the term that is being defined or uses outcomes to define the term. Alvesson (2020) observed that tautologies have been used seemingly tactfully in leadership research to guarantee "valid" and "positive" results. Examples of this are found throughout conceptualizations of transformational leadership (see Table 1). Thus, a conceptualization of transformational leadership is needed that is defined by necessary and sufficient attributes rather than its effects.

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
sections.header_raw: Conflation of transformational leader behaviors with follower evaluations
sections.header_norm: conflation of transformational leader behaviors with follower evaluations
paragraphs: Effective theory building and testing is contingent, in part, on clearly delineating concepts and avoiding theoretical conflation (Shepherd & Suddaby, 2017). A recurring concern expressed in the leadership literature is that scholars are continuing to conflate leader (follower) behaviors with evaluations of those same behaviors (Alvesson, 2020;Antonakis et al., 2016;Day, 2014;Fischer et al., 2020). In other words, scholars are developing or proposing to test theory where leader behavior is an exogenous focal concept (x 1 ), but empirically their measures are based on questionnaires that can tap into a host of endogenous concepts (y i ), including perceptions, evaluations, values, as well as behaviors (Banks, Fischer, et al., 2021). Relatedly, evidence from past reviews demonstrated that many studies do not address the level of analysis of interest (theoretically or empiri-cally) resulting in misalignment between theory and methodology (Yammarino et al., 2005), although this trend has improved overtime (Dionne et al., 2014).

Behaviors can be conceptualized here as "the internally coordinated responses (actions or inactions) of whole living organisms (individuals or groups) to internal and/or external stimuli, excluding responses more easily understood as developmental changes" (Levitis et al., 2009;p. 103). Evaluations, on the other hand, capture followers' psychological state (i.e., primarily assessments, but also attitudes, perceptions, preferences, and feelings; Fischer et al., 2020). Interestingly, in a recent systematic review of 2,338 variables in organizational behavior and leadership research, only 3% of the variables measured were behavioral in nature (only 19% of studies included one or more behavioral measures; Banks, Woznyj, et al., 2021). Past studies on transformational leadership have certainly participated in the conflation of leader (follower) behaviors and evaluations of those behaviors. This is problematic for several reasons.

First, evaluations of behaviors as proxies for behaviors (x 1 ≠ y 1 ) leads to weak theory development and testing can result in an inaccurate (or misleading) accumulation of knowledge. For instance, if a man in a leadership role challenges a follower to seek different perspectives (a way perhaps to transform the follower's thinking), he might be evaluated differently than a woman who engages in the same behavior because of societal role expectations (Eagly & Johnson, 1990;Eagly & Koenig, 2021). This is theoretically interesting to know as both the man and the woman are engaging in the same behavior but are perhaps being evaluated differently. By using evaluations of behaviors as proxies for behaviors, we are unable to confidently develop theories to inform evidence-based practice.

Second, in order to train leaders to be transformational, we must actually understand what behaviors cause the desired outcomes. If the transformational leadership literature is based exclusively on evaluations of leaders' behaviors (an endogenous outcome), we fail to have a theoretical or empirical understanding of what exogenous behaviors caused those evaluations. Past meta-analytic reviews are based entirely on evaluations of TLBs (y 1 ), and the correlations reported are with other evaluative variables, such as other self-reports (e.g., job satisfaction, counterproductive work behavior; y 2 ) or supervisor evaluations of follower behaviors (y 3 ) (Banks, Gooty et al., 2018), rather than follower behavior. Bono and Judge (2004) noted, "as we are aware of no field studies that used behavioral (as opposed to perceptual) measures of transformational leadership, it is hard to know what effect using more rigorous measures might have had on the results" (p. 907). Hence, such meta-analytic reviews are correlating a number of endogenous variables (e.g., y 1 → y 2 ), which prevents causal inferences due to a lack of temporal precedent and an inability to rule out alternative explanations. Moreover, because of the conflation of behaviors and evaluations, it is not clear what specific behaviors leaders should be taught in training.

Third, there can be biases in objective behavioral measures as well as questionnaires (Banks, Fischer, et al., 2021;Banks, Woznyj, et al., 2021). Consequently, approaches that triangulate on the phenomenon of interest will be superior as each approach complements the other in terms of mitigating bias. That is, studies which build and test theory around behaviors and evaluations are advantageous over studies which theoretically neglect one or the other. Similarly, empirically investigating both concepts lead to a stronger body of evidence to understand the phenomenon of interest. It is not clear that current approaches to measuring transformational leadership are capturing much more than whether or not followers simply like their leader (Yammarino et al., 2020).

Ultimately, for the transformational leadership literature to advance, we must understand what behaviors leaders engage in to understand their effects. Research that has been able to delineate specific leader behaviors (e.g., charismatic leader tactics, destructive leadership behaviors, and ethical leader behaviors; Antonakis et al., 2016;Banks, Fischer, et al., 2021;Banks, Ross, et al., 2021;Banks, Woznyj, et al., 2021;Ernst et al., 2021;Fischer et al., 2021) has allowed for stronger causal inferences to be drawn between follower evaluations, leader attributes, and important outcome variables (Antonakis et al., 2011). Identifying specific behaviors allows for easier implementation of experimental designs to determine the causal relationships between important variables of interest (Podsakoff & Podsakoff, 2019). Hence, the current work ultimately seeks to define, identify, and experimentally manipulate TLBs in studies containing important outcome variables.

By using evaluations of behaviors as proxies for behaviors, we are unable to confidently develop theories to inform evidence-based practice.

Second, in order to train leaders to be transformational, we must actually understand what behaviors cause the desired outcomes. If the transformational leadership literature is based exclusively on evaluations of leaders' behaviors (an endogenous outcome), we fail to have a theoretical or empirical understanding of what exogenous behaviors caused those evaluations. Past meta-analytic reviews are based entirely on evaluations of TLBs (y 1 ), and the correlations reported are with other evaluative variables, such as other self-reports (e.g., job satisfaction, counterproductive work behavior; y 2 ) or supervisor evaluations of follower behaviors (y 3 ) (Banks, Gooty et al., 2018), rather than follower behavior. Bono and Judge (2004) noted, "as we are aware of no field studies that used behavioral (as opposed to perceptual) measures of transformational leadership, it is hard to know what effect using more rigorous measures might have had on the results" (p. 907). Hence, such meta-analytic reviews are correlating a number of endogenous variables (e.g., y 1 → y 2 ), which prevents causal inferences due to a lack of temporal precedent and an inability to rule out alternative explanations. Moreover, because of the conflation of behaviors and evaluations, it is not clear what specific behaviors leaders should be taught in training.

Third, there can be biases in objective behavioral measures as well as questionnaires (Banks, Fischer, et al., 2021;Banks, Woznyj, et al., 2021). Consequently, approaches that triangulate on the phenomenon of interest will be superior as each approach complements the other in terms of mitigating bias. That is, studies which build and test theory around behaviors and evaluations are advantageous over studies which theoretically neglect one or the other. Similarly, empirically investigating both concepts lead to a stronger body of evidence to understand the phenomenon of interest. It is not clear that current approaches to measuring transformational leadership are capturing much more than whether or not followers simply like their leader (Yammarino et al., 2020).

Ultimately, for the transformational leadership literature to advance, we must understand what behaviors leaders engage in to understand their effects. Research that has been able to delineate specific leader behaviors (e.g., charismatic leader tactics, destructive leadership behaviors, and ethical leader behaviors; Antonakis et al., 2016;Banks, Fischer, et al., 2021;Banks, Ross, et al., 2021;Banks, Woznyj, et al., 2021;Ernst et al., 2021;Fischer et al., 2021) has allowed for stronger causal inferences to be drawn between follower evaluations, leader attributes, and important outcome variables (Antonakis et al., 2011). Identifying specific behaviors allows for easier implementation of experimental designs to determine the causal relationships between important variables of interest (Podsakoff & Podsakoff, 2019). Hence, the current work ultimately seeks to define, identify, and experimentally manipulate TLBs in studies containing important outcome variables.
sections.preview_head: Effective theory building and testing is contingent, in part, on clearly delineating concepts and avoiding theoretical conflation (Shepherd & Suddaby, 2017). A recurring concern expressed in the leadership literature is that scholars are continuing to conflate leader (follower) behaviors with evaluations of those same behaviors (Alvesson, 2020;Antonakis et al., 2016;Day, 2014;Fischer et al., 2020). In other words, scholars are developing or proposing to test theory where leader behavior is an exogenous focal concept (x 1 ), but empirically their measures are based on questionnaires that can tap into a host of endogenous concepts (y i ), including perceptions, evaluations, values, as well as behaviors (Banks, Fischer, et al., 2021).

Identifying specific behaviors allows for easier implementation of experimental designs to determine the causal relationships between important variables of interest (Podsakoff & Podsakoff, 2019). Hence, the current work ultimately seeks to define, identify, and experimentally manipulate TLBs in studies containing important outcome variables.

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: Numerous calls have been made for transformational leadership research to go back to the drawing board (Alvesson & Einola, 2019;Siangchokyoo et al., 2020;Van Knippenberg & Sitkin, 2013), yet these calls remain unanswered. In Study 1, we took transformational leadership back to the drawing board by reframing it from a signaling theory perspective to guide the needed course correction. Signaling theory allows for a clear delineation between behaviors and evaluations of those behaviors, and provides a theoretical explanation for the causal relationship between behaviors and related outcomes. Additionally, we investigated previous conceptualizations of transformational leadership with the goal of developing a reconceptualization that could be more rigorously studied.

sections.header_raw: Transformational leader behaviors and signaling theory
sections.header_norm: transformational leader behaviors and signaling theory
paragraphs: Signaling theory has been applied across both the natural and social sciences (Attenborough, 2018;Dawkins, 1976;Gleick, 2011) and is fundamentally concerned with the reduction of information asymmetry between two parties (Bergh et al., 2019;Spence, 2002). In this context, signals are defined as behaviors that leaders engage in that are, in part, designed to communicate and thus reduce informa-tion asymmetries necessary to create change in followers (Spence, 1973(Spence, , 2002)). Humans evolved verbal and non-verbal means for signaling information to each other through the use of language and body movements, such as smiling, raising one's eyebrows, or speaking (Attenborough, 2018;Gleick, 2011). Signals are also used by leaders to create social influence with followers (Bastardoz & Van Vugt, 2019).

In a leadership context, signaling encompasses all the possible cues leaders actively or passively demonstrate (i.e., behavioral action or inaction Levitis et al., 2009) that provide the basis for followers' evaluations of the leader (Reh et al., 2017). That is, individuals who wish to be evaluated as a transformational leader can demonstrate the necessary attributes through behaviors and the communication of values (Grabo et al., 2017). There are a number of factors that contribute to the reliability and strength of these signals including credibility, visibility, frequency, value congruence between leaders-followers (Connelly et al., 2011) as well as the associated costs of signaling (e.g., production costs, developmental costs, and maintenance costs; Searcy & Nowicki, 2005).

Leaders can select when and how to signal, signal frequency, and can ensure that there is a fit between the signals sent and the values or goals of followers. Leaders may engage in both "cheap" signaling (e.g., simply communicating a value before having displayed a commitment to that value) or more costly signals, such as investing precious time developing followers. Leading by example may be an especially costly signal for a leader (Hermalin, 1998), but is a way for leaders to model desired behaviors and to facilitate social learning. Regardless of the cost of the signal, followers evaluate the signaling and the information conveyed (Hermalin, 1998).

TLBs likely reduce information asymmetries and create social influence through a number of signaling approaches, with the ultimate goal of developing and changing the follower and their behaviors. perienced athlete), or information about an environment or task that needs to be accomplished. Leaders also hold private information regarding their identity orientation. According to Brewer and Gardner (1996), individuals may adopt a personal, relational, or collective identity orientation. The difference among these three orientations is how the focal individual defines themselves (Flynn, 2005). First, those who define themselves as a unique individual reflect a personal identity orientation. Second, those who define themselves as a member of an interpersonal relationship reflect a relational identity orientation. Finally, those who define themselves as a member of a social group (e.g., team, organization, industry) reflect a collective identity orientation. Leaders can signal their current mindset which is not necessarily fixed. Such information could be particularly important for followers when selecting a leader (Bastardoz & Van Vugt, 2019) as this could signal the extent to which a leader is interested in their personal goals compared to collective goals (i.e., what is mutually beneficial for the group).

Reframing transformational leadership from a signaling theory perspective starts the process of addressing the previously mentioned shortcomings (e.g., definitional issues, conflation of behaviors and evaluations, as well as subsequent measurement issues due to the overuse of questionnaires). Such an approach allows for transformational leadership to be defined and measured more objectively and precisely. Beyond that, signaling theory provides the theoretical framework for exploring what leader behaviors are evaluated as transformational, how TLBs influence followers, and why a follower would want to follow a transformational leader. Next, we turn to our exploration of previous conceptualizations of transformational leadership to identify the attributes necessary for signaling that a leader is transformational.

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: For this review and reconceptualization study, we followed Podsakoff and colleagues' (2016) recommendations for creating better concept definitions in the organizational sciences. This approach involves a four-step process for systematically reviewing previous conceptualizations of transformational leadership, identifying key attributes and behaviors of transformational leadership, and developing an updated conceptualization of TLBs. Ultimately, this process, as well as the reframing from a signaling theory perspective, is critical to the theoretical and empirical course correction for transformational leadership.

Step 1 involved identifying possible attributes of transformational leadership using multiple techniques. First, we searched Google Scholar for "transformational leadership" and "measure" or "scale" or "questionnaire" until the results became highly redundant or irrelevant. Given our interest in both previous conceptualizations of transformational leadership as well as the misalignment between theory and methods, we focused our search on measures of transformational leadership. This approach allowed for the identification of the most popular conceptualization-measure pairs of transformational leadership so that we could assess for both definitional issues as well as the misalignment between theory and methods throughout the literature. We also conducted a supplemental review of all the primary studies included in past meta-analytic reviews of transformational leadership (e.g., Barlow, 2013;Harms & Credé, 2010;Judge & Piccolo, 2004, Wang et al., 2011). In doing so, we made sure to achieve saturation by continuing the search until we found no new information and the definitions became redundant (Becker, Ellevold, & Stamp, 2008). Based on other reviews of transformational leadership conceptually (e.g., Siangchokyoo et al., 2020), we believe a different search strategy would have led to the same conclusions and had the same implications for the four studies we completed. The most common definitions identified through our search can be found in Table 1.

In Step 2, our team reviewed each definition to determine and summarize the key attributes identified in the definitions. In doing so, we identified those attributes that were necessary and sufficient for transformational leadership.

Step 3 involved taking a deeper dive into the necessary attributes of TLBs. Lastly, Step 4 consisted of developing a new conceptual definition that can be more rigorously tested, which we do in later studies.

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: Step 1: Identify potential attributes

Our search resulted in 53 articles containing a conceptualization of transformational leadership. Given the redundancy of conceptualizations presented in the majority of these articles, our final list consisted of the six primary conceptualization-measurement pairs that were found in these articles (see Table 1). The two primary attributes of transformational leadership that emerged from the conceptualizations (see "Implied Attributes" in Table 1) were developmental and prosocial.
Step 2: Organize attributes and identify necessary and sufficient ones

The attribute 'developmental' emerged consistently both directly as well as through comparable attributes such as generating awareness in team members, satisfying higher needs, and offering intellectual stimulation. Relatedly, the importance of 'prosocial behavior' was apparent through components such as encouraging team members to look beyond their self-interest for the good of the group. Consequently, we determined during this step that developmental and prosocial behaviors are necessary attributes of TLBs that signal a transformational leader.
Step 3: Develop a preliminary definition of the Concept.

In the following section, we begin working toward a preliminary definition of TLBs by considering the intentions of each attribute: developmental behavior and prosocial behavior. To do so, we expand upon each attribute and then differentiate these attributes from other leader behavior concepts.

Development. In order for development to occur, leaders must exercise a delicate balance of support and challenge to an individual's (or individuals') thought process (Kegan & Lahey, 2001). Leaders can be supportive in numerous ways such as offering career counseling advice (Bass, 1985), guiding followers to growth experiences that support their development (Amundsen & Martinsen, 2014), and increasing a follower's sense of self-worth (Dansereau et al., 1995). Relatedly, leaders can challenge followers by delegating responsibilities that require them to act in a way that might conflict with their preferred action (Valcea et al., 2011) and by forcing followers to think about problems differently through intellectual stimulation (Rafferty & Griffin, 2004). Importantly, each of these actions benefit from individualized consideration by the leader.

Individualized consideration involves a leader addressing the differences across individuals and the development of those individuals (Avolio & Bass, 1995a). Individualized consideration pertains to the "individual" or "individual within a (leader-follower) dyad" (Dansereau et al., 1995). Importantly, individualized consideration may be a particularly effective signal as it is tailored to the follower and therefore may be a costlier signal due to the required resources (e.g., time and thoughtfulness) in determining the appropriate course of action for the follower.

Developmental behaviors can also be conceptualized at the group and organizational level. At the group level, the leader must also consider appropriate actions for the group's development. For instance, is the group ready for a team dynamics workshop, or will they evaluate this as the leader signaling that they are dysfunctional and need assistance? Similarly, at the organization level, the leader must determine the appropriate actions that are most developmental for that organization. For instance, the executive team might require managers to have regular performance development conversations with their subordinates to signal a companywide commitment to developing its employees. Importantly, the leader is required to tailor the developmental behaviors regardless of the level.

Prosocial Behavior. Brief and Motowidlo (1986) defined prosocial behaviors as acts that promote or protect the welfare of stakeholders. These behaviors can be distinguished along four key dimensions: the genesis, target, goal, and resource of the behavior (Bolino & Grant, 2016). First, the genesis of the behavior can be either reactive or proactive (Grant & Ashford, 2008;Klotz & Bolino, 2013). The second dimension, the target of the behavior, highlights the scope of the intended recipient, which could range from individuals to entire organizations (Williams & Anderson, 1991). Third, the goal of the behaviors can be affiliative or challenging (Van Dyne et al., 1995). Affiliative behaviors are those related to helping and compliance, which often maintain or promote the status quo. Challenging behaviors refer to those that provide different perspectives and seek to alter and change the status quo. Fourth, prosocial behaviors draw on a span of resources, from personal to social and informational (e.g., Foa, 1993). For instance, knowledge sharing draws upon informational resources of insights and skills. These four dimensions of prosocial behaviors should be considered when identifying transformational leader behaviors.

Referring back to Brief and Motowidlo's definition, prosocial behaviors can be acts directed towards stakeholders at different levels. For instance, a manager might offer a subordinate a promotion to advance their position in the organization. Such an act may not only benefit the individual, but it may also benefit a group if the individual receiving the promotion is an effective leader that will improve the team. Relatedly, a manager or executive might promote the welfare of a group or organization by acquiring talent to advance organizational goals. It is worth noting that in some instances a stakeholder may be negatively impacted, directly or indirectly, by a prosocial behavior (Brief & Motowidlo, 1986). For instance, although one individual may benefit from receiving a promotion, another individual may be negatively impacted by not receiving this opportunity to advance their career. Along the same lines, groups and organizations may suffer when top talent is acquired by a different group or team. Importantly, however, prosocial behaviors are acts that promote or protect the welfare of stakeholders at any of these levels.

Core, Related, and Distinct Attributes. In this subsection, we contrast the core attributes of TLBs, developmental and prosocial behavior, with other leadership concepts and related attributes (see Table 2 for a summary of the overlapping and distinct attributes). To start, we consider laissez-faire behavior and transactional leadership. Given that laissez-faire behavior is the absence of leadership, these two styles are entirely distinct. When we look at transactional leadership, we begin to see slightly more overlap. That is, both transactional and transformational leadership can involve rewards for followers. However, transformational leadership only encompasses rewards related to follower development and promoting their welfare, whereas transactional leadership can involve any reward that motivates a follower to accomplish a goal. Next, we shift toward contrasting transformational leadership with charismatic leadership tactics (CLTs; Antonakis et al., 2016) and ethical leader behaviors (ELBs, Banks, Fischer, et al., 2021;Banks, Ross, et al., 2021), both of which have been reframed from a signaling theory perspective.

The core attributes of charismatic leadership are value-based, symbolic, and emotion-laden (Antonakis et al., 2016). Although TLBs can contain values and symbols, the scope of the values and symbols used is not as expansive as CLTs. That is, TLBs are concerned with values related specifically to developmental and prosocial behavior compared to CLTs which are concerned with any values that are appealing to fol- for pay or time off) to underlie the arrangements for explicit or implicit agreement on goals to be reached to obtain the desired rewards or behavior (Bass, 1981(Bass, , 1985(Bass, , 1997)).

▪ Implicit or explicit goals can be beneficial for the group ▪ Transactional leadership involves any rewards that motivate followers to accomplish a goal TLBs only signal tailored development and prosocial value as a potential reward 5. Empowering Leadership: "a process of sharing power, and allocating autonomy and responsibilities to followers, teams, or collectives through a specific set of leader behaviors for employees to enhance internal motivation and achieve work success (Ahearne et al., 2005;Amundsen & Martinsen, 2014;Arnold et al., 2000;Sharma & Kirkman, 2015;Sims et al., 2009;Strauss, 1964)" (Cheong et al. 2019, p. 34).

▪ Leverage empowering behaviors to develop followers ▪ Empowering leadership requires allocating autonomy and decision making, and is uni-directional TLBs can be enacted that do not involve allocating autonomy nor decision making, and are multi-directional 6. Organizational Citizenship Behavior: "individual behavior that is discretionary, not directly or explicitly recognized by the formal reward system, and that in the aggregate promotes the effective functioning of the organization" (Organ, 1988, p. 4)

Prosocial behavior ▪ OCBs may be unobservable by others and done purely to promote organizational functioning rather than for social influence TLBs are always observable and a means of creating social influence 7. Transformational Leader Behaviors: Leader signaling through developmental and prosocial behaviors tailored for each unique stakeholder (e.g., person, dyad, group, organization). (Current manuscript)

lowers. Additionally, emotions may be leveraged in TLBs but are not required for the enactment of them. ELBs contain the attribute moral emotions (Banks, Fischer, et al., 2021) which, too, is not a necessary component of TLBs. Moral emotions may be leveraged through TLBs (e.g., developing a follower's emotional response to an issue), but such expressions are not required (for review of moral emotions see Sy et al., 2018). ELBs also contain the enactment of prosocial values, which is related to prosocial behavior. The primary difference between the prosocial behaviors of TLBs and those of ELBs is again the scope of the value. ELBs are concerned with far reaching values such as benevolence and universalism (i.e., considering the greater implications for all stakeholders). TLBs, however, do not necessarily require the consideration of all stakeholders (i.e., universalism); rather, they require tailored consideration for the party of interest.

Closely related to prosocial behavior are organizational citizenship behaviors (OCBs). OCBs capture the informal acts of cooperation and contributions that are rendered as a function of job satisfaction and perceived fairness (Organ, 2018). Conceptually, OCBs are thought of as being within the concept of prosocial behavior. Thus, there is overlap between the concept of TLBs and OCBs. However, TLBs are observable and a means to create social influence whereas the same is not necessarily true for OCBs. For example, one may engage in OCBs even when no one else can observe this behavior (e.g., privately organizing materials needed for a client meeting). Conversely, for TLBs to create social influence they must be observable behaviors on the part of the leader.

Looking toward conceptualizations of empowering leadership, Cheong et al.'s (2019) review included a summation of previous definitions, resulting in "a process of sharing power, and allocating autonomy and responsibilities to followers, teams, or collectives through a specific set of leader behaviors for employees to enhance internal motivation and achieve work success (Ahearne et al., 2005;Amundsen & Martinsen, 2014;Arnold et al., 2000;Sharma & Kirkman, 2015;Sims et al., 2009;Strauss, 1964)" (p. 34). Two important distinctions can be made between empowering leader behaviors and TLBs. First, although providing autonomy to team members and involving them in decision-making are both developmental leader behaviors, they are not necessary components of TLBs. That is, a leader can enact a developmental behavior that does not involve autonomy nor decisionmaking. For instance, a leader can provide a different perspective to develop a follower's ability to evaluate a situation. However, the follower may not have any autonomy nor decision-making power in the situation. Second, although empowering and delegating behaviors requires hierarchical positioning and authority, TLBs can be utilized regardless of hierarchical positioning. A TLB enacted by someone with hierarchical status and authority may be more likely to be received by others, but it is not a necessary component of its conceptualization. Thus, although TLBs share overlap with CLTs, ELBs, and empowering leader behaviors, there are contrasts to be made for each.
Step 4: Refining the conceptual definition

From the above exploration, we suggest that tailored developmental and prosocial behaviors are necessary components of TLBs, with signaling theory serving as the theoretical framework. Consequently, when combined, tailored developmental and prosocial behaviors are sufficient to serve as signals of TLBs. Thus, we define TLB as:
Leader signaling through developmental and prosocial behaviors tailored for each unique stakeholder (e.g., person, dyad, group, organization).

It is worth noting that in some instances a stakeholder may be negatively impacted, directly or indirectly, by a prosocial behavior (Brief & Motowidlo, 1986). For instance, although one individual may benefit from receiving a promotion, another individual may be negatively impacted by not receiving this opportunity to advance their career. Along the same lines, groups and organizations may suffer when top talent is acquired by a different group or team. Importantly, however, prosocial behaviors are acts that promote or protect the welfare of stakeholders at any of these levels.

Core, Related, and Distinct Attributes. In this subsection, we contrast the core attributes of TLBs, developmental and prosocial behavior, with other leadership concepts and related attributes (see Table 2 for a summary of the overlapping and distinct attributes). To start, we consider laissez-faire behavior and transactional leadership. Given that laissez-faire behavior is the absence of leadership, these two styles are entirely distinct. When we look at transactional leadership, we begin to see slightly more overlap. That is, both transactional and transformational leadership can involve rewards for followers. However, transformational leadership only encompasses rewards related to follower development and promoting their welfare, whereas transactional leadership can involve any reward that motivates a follower to accomplish a goal. Next, we shift toward contrasting transformational leadership with charismatic leadership tactics (CLTs; Antonakis et al., 2016) and ethical leader behaviors (ELBs, Banks, Fischer, et al., 2021;Banks, Ross, et al., 2021), both of which have been reframed from a signaling theory perspective.

The core attributes of charismatic leadership are value-based, symbolic, and emotion-laden (Antonakis et al., 2016). Although TLBs can contain values and symbols, the scope of the values and symbols used is not as expansive as CLTs. That is, TLBs are concerned with values related specifically to developmental and prosocial behavior compared to CLTs which are concerned with any values that are appealing to fol- for pay or time off) to underlie the arrangements for explicit or implicit agreement on goals to be reached to obtain the desired rewards or behavior (Bass, 1981(Bass, , 1985(Bass, , 1997)).

▪ Implicit or explicit goals can be beneficial for the group ▪ Transactional leadership involves any rewards that motivate followers to accomplish a goal TLBs only signal tailored development and prosocial value as a potential reward 5. Empowering Leadership: "a process of sharing power, and allocating autonomy and responsibilities to followers, teams, or collectives through a specific set of leader behaviors for employees to enhance internal motivation and achieve work success (Ahearne et al., 2005;Amundsen & Martinsen, 2014;Arnold et al., 2000;Sharma & Kirkman, 2015;Sims et al., 2009;Strauss, 1964)" (Cheong et al. 2019, p. 34).

▪ Leverage empowering behaviors to develop followers ▪ Empowering leadership requires allocating autonomy and decision making, and is uni-directional TLBs can be enacted that do not involve allocating autonomy nor decision making, and are multi-directional 6. Organizational Citizenship Behavior: "individual behavior that is discretionary, not directly or explicitly recognized by the formal reward system, and that in the aggregate promotes the effective functioning of the organization" (Organ, 1988, p. 4)

Prosocial behavior ▪ OCBs may be unobservable by others and done purely to promote organizational functioning rather than for social influence TLBs are always observable and a means of creating social influence 7. Transformational Leader Behaviors: Leader signaling through developmental and prosocial behaviors tailored for each unique stakeholder (e.g., person, dyad, group, organization). (Current manuscript)

lowers. Additionally, emotions may be leveraged in TLBs but are not required for the enactment of them. ELBs contain the attribute moral emotions (Banks, Fischer, et al., 2021) which, too, is not a necessary component of TLBs. Moral emotions may be leveraged through TLBs (e.g., developing a follower's emotional response to an issue), but such expressions are not required (for review of moral emotions see Sy et al., 2018). ELBs also contain the enactment of prosocial values, which is related to prosocial behavior. The primary difference between the prosocial behaviors of TLBs and those of ELBs is again the scope of the value. ELBs are concerned with far reaching values such as benevolence and universalism (i.e., considering the greater implications for all stakeholders). TLBs, however, do not necessarily require the consideration of all stakeholders (i.e., universalism); rather, they require tailored consideration for the party of interest.

Closely related to prosocial behavior are organizational citizenship behaviors (OCBs). OCBs capture the informal acts of cooperation and contributions that are rendered as a function of job satisfaction and perceived fairness (Organ, 2018). Conceptually, OCBs are thought of as being within the concept of prosocial behavior. Thus, there is overlap between the concept of TLBs and OCBs. However, TLBs are observable and a means to create social influence whereas the same is not necessarily true for OCBs. For example, one may engage in OCBs even when no one else can observe this behavior (e.g., privately organizing materials needed for a client meeting). Conversely, for TLBs to create social influence they must be observable behaviors on the part of the leader.

Looking toward conceptualizations of empowering leadership, Cheong et al.'s (2019) review included a summation of previous definitions, resulting in "a process of sharing power, and allocating autonomy and responsibilities to followers, teams, or collectives through a specific set of leader behaviors for employees to enhance internal motivation and achieve work success (Ahearne et al., 2005;Amundsen & Martinsen, 2014;Arnold et al., 2000;Sharma & Kirkman, 2015;Sims et al., 2009;Strauss, 1964)" (p. 34). Two important distinctions can be made between empowering leader behaviors and TLBs. First, although providing autonomy to team members and involving them in decision-making are both developmental leader behaviors, they are not necessary components of TLBs. That is, a leader can enact a developmental behavior that does not involve autonomy nor decisionmaking. For instance, a leader can provide a different perspective to develop a follower's ability to evaluate a situation. However, the follower may not have any autonomy nor decision-making power in the situation. Second, although empowering and delegating behaviors requires hierarchical positioning and authority, TLBs can be utilized regardless of hierarchical positioning. A TLB enacted by someone with hierarchical status and authority may be more likely to be received by others, but it is not a necessary component of its conceptualization. Thus, although TLBs share overlap with CLTs, ELBs, and empowering leader behaviors, there are contrasts to be made for each.

Step 4: Refining the conceptual definition

From the above exploration, we suggest that tailored developmental and prosocial behaviors are necessary components of TLBs, with signaling theory serving as the theoretical framework. Consequently, when combined, tailored developmental and prosocial behaviors are sufficient to serve as signals of TLBs. Thus, we define TLB as:

Leader signaling through developmental and prosocial behaviors tailored for each unique stakeholder (e.g., person, dyad, group, organization).

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: Next, we seek to identify a taxonomy of leader behaviors to help address measurement issues and the heavy dependence on question-naires within the transformational leadership literature. Questionnaires often contain cognitive abstractions of attributes that are used to measure leadership (Fischer et al., 2020). In doing so, they capture broader, more ambiguous concepts rather than concrete behaviors (Van Quaquebeke & Felps, 2018). Although these measurement tools have certainly advanced a large quantity of research on transformational leadership, numerous critiques have suggested that they have had minimal and, at times, adverse consequences for the quality of the concept (Alvesson & Einola, 2019;Van Knippenberg & Sitkin, 2013). A specific example is that transformational leadership scores on the Multifactor Leadership Questionnaire (MLQ) have a large magnitude relation with authentic leadership (ρ =. 72; Banks et al., 2016), transactional leadership (ρ = .80; Hoch et al., 2018), and ethical leadership (ρ = .70; Judge & Piccolo, 2004) indicating the potential for construct redundancy.
Given these revealing critiques, one might question why such tools are still widely used to measure transformational leadership. To which, Day responded:

"Questionnaires remain a popular (if misguided) approach to studying leadership. If you design and publish a brief, easy-to-administer survey questionnaire, there is little doubt that researchers will use it. But, we should not lose sight of the fact that a map is not the territory, and simply labeling a questionnaire as a measure of 'leadership [or transformational leadership] measure' does not mean that it actually measures leadership [or transformational leadership]" (2014, p. 862).

Questionnaires can be heavily influenced by the evaluator's (i.e., the one completing the questionnaire) biases. Research has suggested that evaluators' reports of transformational leaders are strongly correlated with leadership satisfaction and perceived leadership effectiveness (Banks, Gooty et al., 2018;Brown & Keeping, 2005;Judge & Piccolo, 2004). The implications of this research for transformational leadership theory are twofold: 1) followers with transformational leaders rate higher levels of satisfaction and evaluate their leader to be more effective, and/or 2) followers that are satisfied with their leader and deem them to be effective, evaluate their leader highly on questions regarding transformational leadership or any other seemingly positive items. That is, we have been unable to rule out whether transformational leadership questionnaires simply capture the extent to which "I like my boss" (Yammarino et al., 2020). Moreover, the literature as it stands is unable to rule out rater biases (e.g., bias against women in leadership roles; Sturm et al., 2014) or non-objective evaluations (e.g., a highly satisfied follower evaluation) as an alternative explanation regarding the cause of a leader being rated as transformational.

The second study takes an initial step in identifying a specific taxonomy of such behaviors. Just as there are charismatic and ethical leader behaviors (Antonakis et al., 2011;Banks, Fischer, et al., 2021;Banks, Ross, et al., 2021;Ernst et al., 2021), our goal is to gather preliminary evidence for what constitutes TLBs. Such behaviors would serve as signals to observing parties that the leader values development and prosocial behavior and possesses resources that could help transform stakeholders. Thus, through this study we sought to answer:
Research Question 1: What specific and observable behaviors signal transformational leadership to a follower?

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: Given the decades of research on transformational leadership, we took two steps to building a taxonomy of TLBs. First, we drew upon the published literature in a deductive fashion in order to identify specific TLBs. Second, we took an inductive approach, described below. In the first step, prior to data collection, we conducted a review of behaviors that are commonly associated with transformational leadership. This involved the completion of two tasks as a broader part of Study 1 (see the search description above): 1) reviewing transformational leadership scales (e.g., the MLQ) and 2) reviewing definitions of transformational leadership.

However, to answer our research question, we only considered behaviors that exercise both tailored developmental and prosocial behavior. The goal was to start by identifying a small quantity of behaviors that shared a direct connection with tailored development and prosocial behavior. This stage of creating the taxonomy occurred in conjunction with the review of the literature described in Study 1. Our search and subsequent review yielded three behaviors that included these components: questioning critical assumptions, words of affirmation, and seeking different perspectives. Here, we focus on the specific behaviors themselves. For the purpose of this research, we discuss verbal behaviors but suggest that this work can and needs to be extended to non-verbal behaviors.

The first TLB we identified was questioning critical assumptions (Avolio & Bass, 1995a;Podsakoff et al., 1990). The key elements to this verbal behavior are statements examining the fundamental element(s) of an idea or argument. Importantly, this behavior is developmental for stakeholders by enhancing their ability to examine the critical assumptions of their ideas and processes. It is a prosocial behavior, as the leader is insightfully challenging the target to examine whether a critical assumption is ultimately helpful or harmful. This behavior can signal to the follower(s) the leader's ability to evaluate taken for granted assumptions and subsequently develop the follower(s) by teaching them this skill of thinking critically.

The second TLB is words of affirmation, a verbal behavior that contains content regarding a follower's character strengths (i.e., positive traits reflected through their thoughts, feelings, and behaviors, Park et al., 2004). A strengths-based approach to follower development has been found to be an effective leadership strategy across levels of stakeholders (Clifton & Harter, 2003). By providing words of affirmation, a leader signals that they have identified a strength of the follower(s) and recognizes that this strength can benefit the group. A recent meta-analytic study with participants consisting of employees, students, and community members, found evidence of words of affirmation having a beneficial impact on positive affect, life satisfaction, and increases in flourishing (Schutte & Malouff, 2019). Depending on the follower's stage of development, the identification of strengths may affirm the follower's value while enlightening them of a strength, affirm the follower of their strength and the potential to improve, or affirm how the follower's strength plays a critical role in the success of a team. That is, the leader is ultimately helping the team (i.e., prosocial behavior) by helping a team member to better understand their strengths. Consequently, depending upon the situation, the leader can leverage this behavior in multiple ways by enhancing the follower's awareness of their strength or through empowering the follower to exercise their strength by taking on greater responsibility, either of which are geared toward benefiting the team.

The third TLB, seeking different perspectives, can be expressed as a verbal behavior that involves asking others to express their creative ideas to generate the best solution. In doing so, leaders can develop followers through stimulating innovation and creativity (Avolio & Bass, 1995a). A leader can enact this behavior by seeking feedback from one follower or multiple. Relatedly, seeking perspectives can be leveraged at different points of a follower's development such as through illustrating an effective process for identifying solutions or grooming followers who will soon be responsible for making similar decisions. Through this, leaders are signaling their interest in developing the follower's ability to generate ideas and solutions, their desire to identify the best idea or solution for the group, and that the leader values their followers' perspective (Carmeli et al., 2010;Hirak et al., 2012). Additionally, such perspective seeking provides team members an opportu-nity to voice their ideas and ideally collectively identify a solution that is best for the group (i.e., prosocial). Importantly, signaling appreciation and respect for ideas and perspectives is said to increase inclusivity and, consequently, team performance (Mitchell et al., 2015).

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: Participants. To start the process of inductively identifying TLBs, participants were recruited from a pool of undergraduate psychology students at a university in the southeastern United States. Students at the university are required to participate in research to fulfill a credit requirement for their program. A college student sample was utilized for the experiment involving a commencement address. Ultimately, we suggest that TLBs should work to influence followers regardless of age, similar to other behavioral approaches to leadership, such as CLTs (Antonakis et al., 2016;Ernst et al., 2021). By the point that young adults reach the college stage of their career, it is expected that they have had sufficient exposure to leaders (e.g., coaches, teachers, administrators, managers, as well as leaders covered in the media such as business leaders and politicians). The constant comparative approach also allowed us to compare emerging themes from this dataset relative to the extant literature.

In total, 109 students participated in this study; the average age of the participants was 20 years old, and the sample was 57% women. A sample of approximately 100 participants was estimated to be sufficient to provide for theoretical saturation in the qualitative responses (37,451 total words). Theoretical saturation is the point at which new data generate little, if any, new content or value to the emergent analysis (Glasser & Strauss, 1967). Oftentimes in traditional qualitative research text is collected until themes stabilize. Given the vast body of extant research on transformational leadership, again we used a constant comparative approach, which allowed us to compare emerging themes to the existing literature to reach such a conclusion.

Procedure and Design. Data collection involved providing participants with five open-ended questions that encompassed the concept of transformational leadership (see Study 2 online appendix). The open-ended questions were adapted survey items from two of the four subscales of the MLQ (i.e., individualized consideration and intellectual stimulation). The purpose of adapting the MLQ items was to use these rather generalized leader behavior questions to encourage responses with more specific TLBs. For instance, "The person I am rating spent time teaching and coaching" (MLQ, Avolio & Bass, 1995b) was adapted for this study to "How has your leader developed you through teaching and coaching?" Through this, the respondents would be able to provide both the leader behaviors as well as examples of these behaviors.

Upon agreeing to participate in the study, the students were directed to a Qualtrics survey where they responded to the open-ended questions with specific examples of their experiences with leaders. Participants were asked to respond using at least 50 words in order to ensure higher quality responses which is particularly important for topic modeling (Banks, Woznyj, et al., 2018), the analytic approach we utilized.

Participants. To start the process of inductively identifying TLBs, participants were recruited from a pool of undergraduate psychology students at a university in the southeastern United States. Students at the university are required to participate in research to fulfill a credit requirement for their program. A college student sample was utilized for the experiment involving a commencement address. Ultimately, we suggest that TLBs should work to influence followers regardless of age, similar to other behavioral approaches to leadership, such as CLTs (Antonakis et al., 2016;Ernst et al., 2021). By the point that young adults reach the college stage of their career, it is expected that they have had sufficient exposure to leaders (e.g., coaches, teachers, administrators, managers, as well as leaders covered in the media such as business leaders and politicians). The constant comparative approach also allowed us to compare emerging themes from this dataset relative to the extant literature.

In total, 109 students participated in this study; the average age of the participants was 20 years old, and the sample was 57% women. A sample of approximately 100 participants was estimated to be sufficient to provide for theoretical saturation in the qualitative responses (37,451 total words). Theoretical saturation is the point at which new data generate little, if any, new content or value to the emergent analysis (Glasser & Strauss, 1967). Oftentimes in traditional qualitative research text is collected until themes stabilize. Given the vast body of extant research on transformational leadership, again we used a constant comparative approach, which allowed us to compare emerging themes to the existing literature to reach such a conclusion.

Procedure and Design. Data collection involved providing participants with five open-ended questions that encompassed the concept of transformational leadership (see Study 2 online appendix). The open-ended questions were adapted survey items from two of the four subscales of the MLQ (i.e., individualized consideration and intellectual stimulation). The purpose of adapting the MLQ items was to use these rather generalized leader behavior questions to encourage responses with more specific TLBs. For instance, "The person I am rating spent time teaching and coaching" (MLQ, Avolio & Bass, 1995b) was adapted for this study to "How has your leader developed you through teaching and coaching?" Through this, the respondents would be able to provide both the leader behaviors as well as examples of these behaviors.

Participants were asked to respond using at least 50 words in order to ensure higher quality responses which is particularly important for topic modeling (Banks, Woznyj, et al., 2018), the analytic approach we utilized.

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: To address Research Question 1, we began by conducting a short thematic analysis by coding twenty percent of the responses to become familiar with the data (Braun & Clarke, 2006) and to begin identifying potential themes. Then, we used topic modeling to examine a range of topics that emerged from the students' responses. A topic modeling analysis approach was selected for its ability to combine both the efficiency of computer automation as well as the human-input that aids in unpacking the meaning of the text (Banks et al., 2018;Kobayashi et al., 2018;Oswald et al., 2020;Tonidandel et al., 2021). The topic modeling analysis utilizes Latent Dirchlet Allocation (LDA; Blei et al., 2010) to identify and measure hidden (latent) topics within the data. LDA is a well-known algorithm for topic modeling that provides analysts with the ability to use a pre-programmed set of rules to reduce the dimensions of a corpus (set of textual documents; Mohr & Bogdanov, 2013). The key assumption in LDA is that the words that make up a document are samples from a mixture model, where the mixture components are random variables that represent topics (Hannigan et al., 2019).

Before conducting this analysis, we performed standard preprocessing steps such as tokenization, stemming, and the removal of stop words (e.g., also, will, can, every, leader). A Bayesian hierarchical mixture model was applied which draws upon co-occurrence among words in order to determine emerging topics (for review see Banks, Woznyj, et al., 2018). We began the topic modeling process without an a priori number of topics because the purpose of topic modeling is not to correctly estimate the quantity of topics. Instead, the purpose is to identify the highest quality lens through which one can see the data most clearly (DiMaggio et al., 2013). The topics that emerged were examined using the constant comparative method (Glasser & Strauss, 1967), drawing from transformational leadership literature to identify topics that made conceptual sense while maintaining parsimony and maximizing interpretability (Strauss & Corbin, 1990).

The goal of this process was to confirm that the topics emerging from the analysis are both robust and can be connected to the literature. We followed steps outlined by Short et al. (2010) as well as Banks, Woznyj, et al. (2018) for labeling and composing working definitions of the emerging themes. First, an inductive approach was taken to label the emerging latent concepts from the text and to create working definitions. Second, the author team discussed the topics that emerged and the words that co-occurred within them and assigned working labels to the phenomena (Cowan & Fox, 2015). Discussion continued until the research team agreed that the topic labels and working definitions were robust and accurately characterized the data. Questions posed during the discussion included: "How are these topics similar to one another?" "How are these topics different?" and "If they are different, in what way?" (Cowan & Fox, 2015). Finally, the TLB labels and definitions that emerged from the analyzed text were confirmed when it was determined that the research team was satisfied with the definitions and that the themes could be supported with examples from the data. The output of this process can be found in Table 3, which consists of labels for the topics and supporting examples from data and evidence from the literature.

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: Participant responses corroborated the three deductively identified TLBs. The themes of questioning critical assumptions and words of affirmation emerged through numerous responses such as: "She would make us think and back up our responses with evidence and why we thought the way that we did" (question critical assumptions) and "I think it's that support and encouragement that helped me push my limits and develop my ability to think critically" (words of affirmation). Responses regarding seeking different perspectives typically referred to a teacher asking for each students' perspective for pedagogical purposes, with only a few referencing something outside of the class: "…at my old job my manager who was a good leader asked her employees what they think, we knew she had the overall say but a good leader engages with the group and realizes the power they have and use it to benefit the most amount of people" (seeks different perspectives). In addition to providing corroborating evidence for the three deductively identified behaviors, the participant responses also afforded three additional TLBs: teaching life lessons, introducing followers to developmental opportunities, and providing different perspectives to view a problem.

Teaching life lessons can be an effective means of leveraging a story or metaphor to foster development. This TLB is a verbal behavior involving making statements about a lesson or principle applicable to one or more than one area of life. In doing so, signaling to the listener that the leader has valuable resources such as experience and wisdom that can help develop others and determine that which is best for the group. For example, participants said: I always kept this in mind because it doesn't just apply to playing sports but can apply to your job in a team setting or even in a family setting. Not only was he a leader for the sport but also a leader to teaching me important life skills like self-discipline, responsibility, the importance of showing up. As my math professor said before, "If you can teach someone how to solve one question, it means that you truly understand that objective."

A second TLB that emerged from the data was making statements that introduce followers to a developmental opportunity, a verbal behavior that involves encouraging others to step out of their comfort zone for the sake of growth and development. Such behavior signals that the leader is committed to the follower's development, and that they want the individual to succeed. It is through this developmental behavior that a leader might empower their followers by placing them in an uncomfortable position, where they are required to act in a way that might conflict with their preferred action (Valcea et al., 2011). In doing so, presenting the opportunity for a follower to learn important lessons that might allow them to contribute to the welfare of the group. Additionally, such leader behavior might signal that the leader is willing to invest their time and resources to provide the follower with a personalized learning opportunity that is tailored to their specific needs. For example, participants wrote:

She encouraged me to push out of my comfort zone and try new things. …helped me overcome my fears of being in front of people and helped me become the drum major of my high school. He encouraged me to get out of my comfort zone and push myself to try out for drum major and his constant praise and encouragement really helped me succeed in being a leader for my fellow classmates.
He was always putting me in leadership positions.

The final leader behavior identified from the participant responses was presenting different perspectives which is a verbal behavior that includes making statements with ideas or arguments that display different viewpoints on a matter. This behavior can help to develop a follower's problem awareness and problem-solving ability. Additionally, the statements with the leader's different perspectives help challenge the follower to advance their thinking by taking into consideration alternative or contradictory ideas (Bass, 1985). Through all of this, the leader is signaling their ability to analyze an idea or situation from multiple angles in order to derive the best result for the group. Some examples from the data include:
Leaders often seem to inspire or challenge me by pointing out different ways i could evaluate a situation or issue.

They told me how to be in others' shoes to see their point of view, even if my point of view was not wrong. Just to look at a situation or problems at a different angle. which has helped me mature and become a more understanding person…

It is important to note that all of these TLBs can be utilized at different levels of analysis by changing the level of analysis referent. In the following section, we use the individual level to describe each behavior for illustrative purposes.

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
sections.header_raw: The role of TLBs as signals
sections.header_norm: the role of tlbs as signals
paragraphs: We have now presented six TLBs which include seeking different perspectives, questioning critical assumptions, delivering words of affirmation, teaching life lessons, introducing followers to development opportunities, and presenting different perspectives. Unlike past work, this research identified six tangible behaviors (i.e., signals) which are theoretically separated from evaluations of those behaviors. This addresses the past critique that transformational leadership is tautological (i.e., someone is transformational when they have transformed followers). Moreover, these TLBs can be experimentally manipulated, as will be illustrated in Studies 3 and 4 below. Finally, these TLBs can be trained. Past research has focused on follower evaluations measured via the MLQ, leading to a dearth of knowledge regarding how to actually teach someone to transform followers.

Leaders that engage in these behaviors cause positive transformation in followers through social influence. When followers see positive transformation of peers or others, this could certainly attract more followers (facilitating leader emergence) and greater social influence in which followers may be more willing to engage in behaviors directed by the transformational leader. Consistent with signaling theory, individuals who leverage TLBs are seeking to communicate privately held information to followers to facilitate improved task performance and other outcomes. Very specifically, signals are "things one does that are visible and that are in part designed to communicate" (Spence, 2002, p. 434). For example, when leaders question critical assumptions, they signal and draw followers' attention to key elements of an idea, concept, or process. Leaders share private information with followers about how best to examine a problem and to critically evaluate a situation. This type of behavior signals to followers that they too should critically evaluate assumptions.

As another example, delivering statements with words of affirmation is a means for leaders to signal to followers privately held beliefs that they believe in a follower's competence and recognize their strengths. These signals direct followers to think about their strengths and how they may best use their skills. As signaling theory involves the reduction in information asymmetries between a signaler and a receiver, this TLB can share private information that a leader holds in the form of beliefs about a follower. As a final example of the new application of signaling theory, leaders may make statements that present different perspectives. Although followers may hold one perspective, leaders can create transformation by signaling that there is more than one perspective in many situations that could be considered. This can signal that there is more than one way to solve a problem or that at the very least, multiple perspectives should be considered before selecting a solution. This behavior serves as a mechanism to help to transform the behaviors of followers.

This type of behavior signals to followers that they too should critically evaluate assumptions.

As another example, delivering statements with words of affirmation is a means for leaders to signal to followers privately held beliefs that they believe in a follower's competence and recognize their strengths. These signals direct followers to think about their strengths and how they may best use their skills. As signaling theory involves the reduction in information asymmetries between a signaler and a receiver, this TLB can share private information that a leader holds in the form of beliefs about a follower. As a final example of the new application of signaling theory, leaders may make statements that present different perspectives. Although followers may hold one perspective, leaders can create transformation by signaling that there is more than one perspective in many situations that could be considered. This can signal that there is more than one way to solve a problem or that at the very least, multiple perspectives should be considered before selecting a solution. This behavior serves as a mechanism to help to transform the behaviors of followers.

We have now presented six TLBs which include seeking different perspectives, questioning critical assumptions, delivering words of affirmation, teaching life lessons, introducing followers to development opportunities, and presenting different perspectives. Unlike past work, this research identified six tangible behaviors (i.e., signals) which are theoretically separated from evaluations of those behaviors. This addresses the past critique that transformational leadership is tautological (i.e., someone is transformational when they have transformed followers). Past research has focused on follower evaluations measured via the MLQ, leading to a dearth of knowledge regarding how to actually teach someone to transform followers.

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: Study 3 seeks to establish preliminary evidence that the behaviors identified in Study 2 serve as signals to followers by carrying information that the leader is transformational. Importantly, identifying signals that cause followers to evaluate the leader differently is an initial step in the social influence process (Fischer et al. 2020). After establishing this, the next logical step is to then demonstrate a causal connection between leader behaviors and follower behavioral outcomes. It is important to note that the results of Study 3 cannot provide evidence that certain leaders transform followers or their organizations. However, we can make theoretical and practical advancements by using a randomized experimental design, the "gold standard" for testing causality (Antonakis et al., 2010), to examine whether the behaviors identified in Study 2 cause followers to evaluate a leader as more transformational. Hence, we propose to test the initial effects of TLBs on follower evaluations: Hypothesis 1. Signals of transformational leader behaviors cause followers to evaluate a leader as more transformational.

Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: Participants were undergraduate and graduate students contacted through a university research Listserv. Using g*power (Faul et al., 2009), an a priori power analysis, indicated that at least 352 participants were needed (Cohen's d = .30, α = .05, and Power = .80; two-tailed). In total, 416 students participated in the study; no participants were removed from the dataset. The final sample was 54 participants beyond the power analysis sample size due to the unanticipated fast pace at which participants signed up and completed the experiment (the data collection was completed in less than 3 h). The first 100 participants were compensated with a $5 Amazon Gift Card, and each participant was entered in a random drawing to receive one of two $50 Amazon Gift Cards. We did not collect demographic data as such information was not related to any hypothesis or research question. We had no reason to theorize that age or gender plays a role in evaluations for transformational leadership. Though, the student population at the university is 47% female with approximately 25% of all students being under the age of 20, about 62% between the ages of 20-29, and about 13% over 30 years old.
