sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: This between-subjects study involved two student commencement speeches that were presented as if the commencement address was held virtually. Although in the past this scenario may have seemed unrealistic, the conditions of the COVID-19 pandemic had made it quite realistic for a commencement address to be held virtually. Both speeches were delivered by the same actress, a Caucasian woman, aged 25, who maintained the same appearance for both speeches.

In order to ensure that the transformational speech (i.e., experimental condition) was not being compared against a weak or bad condition (i.e., a poison vs. medicine comparison; Lonati et al., 2018), the standard speech (i.e., control condition) was written first and used as a template for the transformational speech. That is, the standard speech was written in a way to capture what a student commencement speaker might realistically say in such a speech. To write the speech, we observed numerous recordings on YouTube of student commencement addresses. The standard speech was made up of 24 sentences and zero TLBs whereas the speech written to be transformational consisted of 21 sentences and 19 TLBs (see Study 3 folder in the OSF online appendix for the speeches). The speeches were similar in length (529 words for the transformational condition, 507 words for the standard condition). Lastly, the speeches were recorded with the university's logo as the background. An objective manipulation check was not added as the study itself is a subjective manipulation check similar to work done by Antonakis et al. (2021). In fact, we would suggest that this subjective manipulation check is perhaps the more important one as our ultimate goal was to know the extent to which the TLBs were manipulated sufficiently to cause differences in evaluations of TLBs.

Participants were randomly assigned to either the transformational condition or the standard condition. After watching their assigned video, participants were asked to rate the speaker using 8-items from two of the four dimensions of the Multifactor Leadership Questionnaire (MLQ; 5X-Short; Avolio & Bass, 1995b). Banks et al. (2017) demonstrated that the other two dimensions, idealized influence and inspirational motivation, map onto Antonakis et al.'s (2016) definition of charismatic leadership. After omitting the two unrelated dimensions, the remaining two consist of four items that assess intellectual stimulation (e.g., "Re-examined critical assumptions to question whether they were appropriate") and four items concerned with individualized consideration (e.g., "Helped me to develop my strengths"). The items were slightly adapted to better fit the context. The most significant changes to the items include changing "of a group" to "of the audience" and "how to complete assignments" to "how to overcome challenges." We do not list the exact items here because the MLQ is proprietary. These changes may be considered as relatively minor based on survey responses from a group of psychometricians (Heggestad et al., 2019). The MLQ uses a 5-point Likert-type frequency scale from 0 = not at all to 4 = frequently, if not always. The reliability estimate of the scale in this study was ω = .85 (Cortina et al., 2020;McNeish, 2018). An evaluation of reliability estimates for each group yields a value of ω = .86 for the control group and ω = .82 for the experimental group.

Although the use of the MLQ was critiqued previously in this manuscript, it was incorporated in this study for two reasons. First, an experimental design limits the introduction of numerous biases that might influence the participant's responses on the MLQ (e.g., being highly satisfied in their relationship with a leader), a primary critique of the MLQ. Second, by eliminating the subdimensions that measure charisma (inspirational motivation and idealized influence), the use of the MLQ no longer conflates transformational leadership and charismatic leadership (Banks et al., 2017). Instead, it measures only intellectual stimulation and individualized consideration, the subdimensions of the MLQ that best capture transformational leadership as it has been reconceptualized in Study 1.

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: An independent-samples t-test was conducted to compare the evaluations of the standard speech and the transformational speech. The results indicate that there was a significant difference in the evaluations of the standard speech group and transformational speech group, (t(4 1 4) = 5.05, p < .001, Cohen's d = .50). That is, the average evaluation score in the standard speech group (n = 212, M = 1.97, SD = 1.22) was significantly lower than the transformational speech group (n = 204, M = 2.38, SD = 1.13).

The results of this study suggest that certain leader behaviors signal to followers the leader's capability to transform the follower. Theoretically, this means that these signals serve as a means to begin to reduce information asymmetry between the follower and the leader (Bergh et al., 2019). That is, the TLBs serve as valuable pieces of information that are likely to be interpreted by followers as indicators that the leader is capable of developing the follower(s) in a way that is beneficial for some collective. This is not to say that the study has provided any evidence supporting that these behaviors actually transform followers. Instead, these signaling behaviors serve as, at minimum, observable triggers in the social influence process inherent in leadership (Fischer et al., 2017). In Study 4, we investigate whether TLBs actually transform followers' behaviors.

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: The first three studies serve to advance the transformational leadership literature beyond the ritualized use of questionnaires by theoretically and empirically separating leader behaviors and follower evaluations. However, transformational leadership is much more than simply being evaluated as transformational. Past meta-analytic reviews have demonstrated that follower evaluations of leaders via the MLQ questionnaire is correlated with follower self-reports of attitudes and supervisor rated job performance. Hence, there is a need to begin to investigate how TLBs can actually cause behavioral outcomes.

Consequently, in this fourth and final study, we seek to show how leaders who demonstrate the TLBs identified in Study 2 can cause a change in follower behaviors. An important element of leadership is social influence. In the case of transformational leadership, the aim is to change or transform follower behavior in ways that will benefit the individual as well as the larger stakeholder group. For instance, followers may have a natural tendency to act in self-interested ways (Kollock, 1998) and may withhold from contributing towards community initiatives even if there could be long term benefits for themselves (e.g., the tragedy of the commons; Hardin, 1968). Leaders hold a unique perspective in their ability to understand the needs of the many as well as individuals. This information asymmetry can be bridged via leader signaling by sending credible signals that could be used to energize and transform follower behavior (Bastardoz & Van Vugt, 2019). For example, encouraging prosocial behavior in the form of financial contributions to a public good. Hence, we propose the following hypothesis:
Hypothesis 2. Leaders who engage in transformational leader behaviors to a greater extent cause followers to make greater financial contributions to the public good.

In the case of transformational leadership, the aim is to change or transform follower behavior in ways that will benefit the individual as well as the larger stakeholder group. For instance, followers may have a natural tendency to act in self-interested ways (Kollock, 1998) and may withhold from contributing towards community initiatives even if there could be long term benefits for themselves (e.g., the tragedy of the commons; Hardin, 1968). Leaders hold a unique perspective in their ability to understand the needs of the many as well as individuals. This information asymmetry can be bridged via leader signaling by sending credible signals that could be used to energize and transform follower behavior (Bastardoz & Van Vugt, 2019). For example, encouraging prosocial behavior in the form of financial contributions to a public good. Hence, we propose the following hypothesis:

Hypothesis 2. Leaders who engage in transformational leader behaviors to a greater extent cause followers to make greater financial contributions to the public good.
sections.preview_head: The first three studies serve to advance the transformational leadership literature beyond the ritualized use of questionnaires by theoretically and empirically separating leader behaviors and follower evaluations. However, transformational leadership is much more than simply being evaluated as transformational. Past meta-analytic reviews have demonstrated that follower evaluations of leaders via the MLQ questionnaire is correlated with follower self-reports of attitudes and supervisor rated job performance. Hence, there is a need to begin to investigate how TLBs can actually cause behavioral outcomes.
sections.preview_tail: Hypothesis 2. Leaders who engage in transformational leader behaviors to a greater extent cause followers to make greater financial contributions to the public good.

The experiment was conducted using oTree (Chen et al., 2016), an open-source and online software for implementing interactive experiments. We slightly adapted the experiment conducted by Antonakis and colleagues (2021) who used a similar design to evaluate the extent to which CLTs cause follower contributions to a public good.

paragraphs: Participants were undergraduate and graduate students from the author team's university. Recruitment was accomplished through a university email listserv as well as through business and psychology courses at the undergraduate and graduate levels. A power analysis yielded a sample size requirement of approximately 77 groups (f 2 = .15, α = .05, power = .80). The final sample size was 80 groups (320 total participants). The average age was 23 years old, 52% of the sample identified as women, 3% identified as non-binary or selected to self-describe, and 47% were Caucasian.

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: Participants were recruited to select a time slot, which provided an opportunity for 8-24 participants to register per time slot. Participants within each time slot were randomly assigned to either a control group or a treatment group and each group contained four participants. All participants participated online and were not aware of the identity of the other players. At the time of their scheduled game time, participants received a link to a Qualtrics survey with written instructions on how to play the game. These instructions were the same for all participants. After reading the instructions, participants were asked to complete a series of knowledge checks to ensure they understood the rules of the game (Antonakis et al., 2021).

Once all four participants in a group completed the knowledge checks, they received a link to begin the game via email. Prior to beginning the game, participants viewed a five-minute instructional video, which served as the experiment's manipulation. The control condition watched a standard speech that explained the importance of contributing to the public good, with no TLBs embedded in the speech. The experimental group viewed a video of a speech with embedded TLBs. All four participants within a single game watched the same instructional video. Participants were not able to advance to begin the game until 5 min had elapsed to ensure the video could not be skipped.

After the completion of the video, the synchronous game began. The game was a public goods game consisting of 10 periods. Each period followed the same format. All participants were allocated a 20point endowment at the start of each period. They could then choose to contribute 0-20 points from their personal endowment into a public goods account. Their remaining points were automatically deposited into their personal account. During each period, the public goods account was multiplied by two and the product then was distributed equally among all four participants. The amount received back from the public goods account was then deposited into the participants' individual personal accounts. This sequence was repeated through all 10 periods of the game. Each period, the participants received a new 20-point endowment and did not have access to their personal account balance. At the end of each period, participants were able to view how many points each player contributed to the public goods account and how much each player received back. At the end of all 10 periods, each player's points in their personal accounts were converted to US Dollars at a rate of 1 point = $.035. All participants received a minimum of $10 and could earn a maximum of $18.

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: The speeches used for the control and treatment condition in this study were adapted from the standard speech developed by Antonakis et al. (2021) in their public goods game experiment. Both speeches in the current study were five minutes, had approximately the same number of words (673 for the control speech and 688 for the transformational speech), and provided the same basic instructional information. The transformational speech included transformational behaviors identified in Study 2, whereas the standard speech was a good quality speech that does not contain TLBs. A Caucasian man, age 45, was recruited to deliver both speeches under identical circumstances.

Both the control (i.e., standard) speech and treatment (i.e., TLB) speech were coded for TLBs. An initial round of coding was conducted by two doctoral students who were not co-authors on the project. The students were blinded to the speech type but were familiar with the process of classifying speech text. The average TLB score was 13.5 for the treatment group compared to 6 for the control group. However, percent agreement was just 62%. Hence, a second round was completed with two different doctoral students that had the same qualifications. This time the two students were trained first on an executive letter to shareholders. The training included coding of text followed by a discussion for norming. The coders then completed independent coding of the speeches used in Study 4. This time the average TLB score was 11 for the treatment speech and .50 for the control speech. Further, reliability improved in the scoring in that percent agreement was 89% and Cohen's Kappa = .78.

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: Our first primary analysis involved examining the average contribution of each participant across the 10 periods and regressing that onto the experimental condition. We were also interested in whether a participant's contribution varied as a function of time period, so we regressed participant contribution on experimental condition, time period, and the interaction. Because the maximum allocation for each session is 20, we anticipated that the response variable would be censored. As a result, we repeated the two analyses above using Tobit regression (Choirat et al., 2017) to account for the censoring (Henningsen, 2017). Finally, across all four analyses described above, cluster robust standard errors were used to account for the nesting of individuals within groups.

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: We summarize the results of the Study 4 experiment in Fig. 1 (for a correlation matrix see Table 5 in Appendix A). There are a number of interesting observations from looking at the graph in Fig. 1. First, the TLB condition (i.e., the treatment) outperformed the control condition across all 10 periods in that the average contribution was larger in the TLB condition. Moreover, in all 10 periods the error bars do not overlap. Hence, one may conclude that there are statistically and practically significant differences between the treatment and the control group. The overall pattern of the TLB condition suggests that although there is some fluctuation in contributions, the average contribution tends to remain relatively stable. Conversely, the control condition begins substantially lower, increases in the middle periods, but then drops significantly in the final three rounds. Thus, participants in the TLB condition made consistently higher contributions to the public good.

Statistically, we began by calculating the simple mean-difference after the first period at the individual level of analysis. Here, we found evidence for a mean-difference between the two groups (t (318) = 3.25; p =.001; Cohen's d = .36). This suggests that the trans-formational leadership condition (n = 160; M = 18.49; SD = 2.97) caused a significant mean-difference in the initial contribution to the public good relative to the standard condition (n = 160; M = 17.08; SD = 4.62). Next, summarized in Table 4, we compared groups using cluster robust standard errors (B = 1.42; SE = .71; p =.05) and using a Tobit model (to account for censoring) along with cluster robust standard errors (B = 3.23; SE = 1.36; p =.02). We did not find support for an interaction between condition and time period. These results mirror those which are depicted in Fig. 1. In summary, we conclude that there was support for Hypothesis 2 in that leaders who engage in TLBs can to a greater extent can cause followers to make greater financial contributions to the public good. That is, the study provides evidence that leaders who engage in TLBs can cause changes in follower behaviors.

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: Transformational leadership has undeniably played a crucial role in advancing leadership research and is arguably the most commonly studied leadership concept (Banks, Gooty et al., 2018;Bono & Judge 2004;van Knippenberg & Sitkin, 2013). However, after 25 years of research, we have only very limited evidence that is not correlational or that captures leader (follower) behavior in the transformational leadership process (Antonakis, 2012). As noted in the introduction of this work, the paucity of advancement stems primarily from 1) a lack of conceptual clarity, 2) conflation of leader behaviors with follower evaluations, and 3) the overreliance of questionnaires that leads to a misalignment between theory and methods. Although this is not the first work to raise concerns over the lack of research on behaviors in leadership research (e.g., Alvesson, 2020;Antonakis et al., 2016;Banks, Fischer, et al., 2021;Day, 2014;Fischer et al., 2020), this work serves as an initial step in addressing the concerns raised for transformational leadership. Consequently, the results of this work have important theoretical as well as practical implications.

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: First, the current work sought to advance transformational leadership by reframing the concept using signaling theory. Signaling theory brings with it a rich history that draws upon other scientific domains (Connelly et al., 2011;Dawkins, 1976;Smith & Harper, 2003), which is a promising sign for future theoretical linkages and causal connections. Thus, the theoretical connection between transformational lead-ership and signaling theory opens the door to a greater conceptual toolbox from which transformational leadership can advance.

Second, this work has generated theoretical advancements by presenting a refined conceptualization of TLB as well as a preliminary list of specific TLBs. Study 1 advanced a clarified conceptual definition of TLBs, and Study 2 identified six leader behaviors: teaching life lessons, introduction to developmental opportunities, providing different perspectives, seeking different perspectives, questioning critical assumptions, and speaking words of affirmation. Study 3 found supporting evidence (Cohen's d = .50) that these six behaviors cause followers to evaluate the leader as transformational. Importantly, the objectivity and observability of these leader behaviors are critical for theory development, as such an approach reduces the degree of bias and discrimination in the development of these theories. For instance, gender bias is a multilevel phenomenon that impacts organizations at all levels as well as organizational research (Hogue & Lord, 2007). Consequently, findings from research studies that do not include objective measures may be influenced by organization wide, systemic biases. Objective measures of leadership reduce the potential for implicit theories of leadership to creep into the findings which could ultimately be used to inform practitioners and policy makers.

Third, Study 4 showed that the TLBs caused followers to contribute more to a public good for their betterment. This occurred across 10 periods of a public good game with financial implications. The effects of TLBs prevailed despite idiosyncrasies in the behaviors of other participants in their group. Consequently, this study addressed both the endogeneity problem as well as the conflation of TLBs with follower evaluations by introducing follower behavior as the outcome of an experiment. In doing so, we bring follower behavior back into transformational leadership.

Fourth, although the primary purpose of the current work was to reframe transformational leadership from the perspective of signaling theory, there are also some important takeaways for practice. The current work provides evidence that TLBs can be objectively observed, which is an important advancement for training and development as well as selection. Traditional evaluations of leader behaviors consisted of questionnaires which are riddled with problems that distort findings. Observable behaviors may help alleviate such biases, allowing for greater objectivity in areas such as selection.

Fifth, there is currently little theoretical and empirical work regarding how leaders can develop TLBs. Traditional empirical studies of transformational leader training involve group training sessions including discussions around ideal leaders, and individual sessions to provide feedback based on questionnaire results (Barling et al., 1996). Although such training may be helpful and serve a purpose, a taxonomy of evidence-based behaviors would be a significantly more effective and efficient approach for teaching effective leadership tactics (e.g., Antonakis et al., 2011). Studies 1 and 2 developed such a taxonomy of TLBs, with Studies 3 and 4 finding evidence for the observability and effectiveness of these behaviors.

Identifying a taxonomy of TLBs allows for actionable learning events that result in greater retention (Kraiger & Ford, 2021). Such an approach draws upon empirically supported educational tactics and incorporates them into workplace learning and development. For instance, instructors can present videos of exemplary TLBs and then have training attendees write and deliver speeches involving these behaviors (Gingerich et al., 2014). In addition to more effective pedagogical tactics, this form of training can also be easily evaluated for its efficacy. Gurdjian et al. (2014) reported repeated findings of companies speaking to the importance of developing leadership skills, but rarely having empirical evidence for the efficacy of their programs that aim to accomplish this. Randomized experiments, such as the designs of Studies 3 and 4, can provide evidence for the efficacy of implementing such behaviors.

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: The current work took a four-study approach in order to triangulate on the phenomenon of interest and minimize limitations. That being said, there are a number of limitations that also serve as opportunities for future research. First, the aim of Studies 1 and 2 was to attempt to make an incremental contribution to the study of transformational leadership by disentangling behaviors from evaluations. We wanted to identify a definition and specific behaviors that caused the answers to common survey items, then to demonstrate in the next two experiments (Studies 3 and 4) that these behaviors cause evaluations and behaviors. Although not a flawless approach, this is a means to strike a balance between acknowledging the existing literature area and still inductively allowing behaviors to emerge.

Second, Study 3 examined whether leader behaviors cause variation in follower evaluations of leadership, and later in Study 4, follower behaviors. That is, we manipulated the TLBs identified in Studies 1 and 2 and showed that the manipulation of those behaviors (x 1 ) caused evaluations of the leader as transformational (y 1 ). This effort then provides a critical contribution over and above the existing literature, which measures y 1 and then correlates it with other concepts (usually all measured via questionnaires, sometimes from the same source at the same time), such as organizational commitment, turnover intentions, etc. (y i ). The TLBs serve as signals that differentially affect followers' beliefs about the speaker's capability to develop people, which then cause the evaluation of the leader. However, this change in beliefs reflects a mediating mechanism. We did not test mediating mechanisms in either Study 3 or 4 as we focused on establishing main effects first and foremost. This does open up a fruitful avenue for future research.

Third, we have modeled our approach to reconceptualizing transformational leadership behavior similar to Antonakis and colleagues, which includes definitional work on charismatic leadership (Antonakis et al., 2016) and demonstrated that manipulation of a set of CLTs can drive a variety of outcomes (Antonakis et al., 2011;Antonakis et al., 2021;Jacquart et al., 2016). We would suggest that looking at contingencies of TLBs and curvilinear effects offer avenues for future research. A very clever way to accomplish this would be to develop a machine learning algorithm that could automatically score videos in which leaders used TLBs and look at patterns in the data. At least for this preliminary work, we sought to demonstrate that a collection of six TLBs could drive evaluations and follower behavior. This approach is theoretically sound in that we looked at how a set of consistent signals sends a powerful message to followers (Connelly et al., 2011).

Fourth, future research should consider the exploration of other dependent variables, such as objective measures of follower behaviors or supervisor subjective evaluations of task performance. Finally, in Study 3 we compared and contrasted the control speech against the treatment speech on CLTs to rule out alternative explanations. The control speech scored 11.97 on CLTs relative to 16.04 for the treatment speech according to the NLP algorithm developed by Garner et al. (2019). We did the same for Study 4 and found an even smaller difference (9.23 CLTs relative to 10.22 for the treatment speech). We certainly think more work could be done to distinguish CLTs, TLBs, and other leader behaviors. For example, future research could explore the extent to which TLBs significantly impact evaluations of TLBs and not evaluations of ELBs or CLTs.

sections.paper_id: Stock et al. - 2023 - Putting leader (follower) behavior back into transformational leadership A theoretical and empirica
paragraphs: Transformational leadership has received a great deal of attention due to the slew of correlational research, but causal and behavioral evidence eludes the field. Consequently, numerous concerns prevent the advancement of theory and evidence-based practice. In this work, we first reconceptualized transformational leadership by reframing from a signaling theory perspective. Next, we presented four studies which advanced a definition of TLBs, a taxonomy of six TLBs, and then demonstrated that the TLBs cause variation in follower evaluations of leadership and follower behaviors. In doing all of this, we started the process of putting leader (follower) behavior back into the research on transformational leadership. More work will be needed to continue to refine the taxonomy of TLBs and to identify important mediating and moderating factors. Thus, the current work provides multiple avenues for future research and practice.
